content.assessment: Admissions-process reform leaves the spatial distribution of tracks (urban/county-seat concentration) and the underlying cohort decline entirely unaddressed; it operates on selection mechanics within existing sites rather than on where those sites are located or how enrollment pools are shrinking. From a demographic standpoint, this proposal is largely orthogonal to cohort trajectory and geography: it neither exacerbates nor relieves the closure pressure facing comprehensive schools, nor does it change the insulation of tracked schools from depopulation. The counterfactual—no admissions reform—would leave the same spatial/cohort pattern intact, meaning this proposal's demographic footprint is essentially neutral.
opportunities: Could be combined with geographic targeting (e.g., differential admissions rules by settlement type) to address the spatial dimension the proposal itself does not touch
strengths: Does not add administrative burden to an already-stressed low-enrollment network undergoing closures
threats: Because admissions-process tweaks do not alter where schools are located, disadvantaged rural and small-settlement families remain structurally excluded from the tracks regardless of exam-design changes
weaknesses: Fails to engage with the geographic concentration of tracks (59 Budapest, 63 provincial cities, 121 small towns, only 8 villages) that structurally shapes who has access to selective admission processes in the first place
